text: All three valves performed satisfactorily through 100 test cycles.
Three types were tested in valve test stands by using reactor-quality salt at 1100 to $1200^{\mathrm{O}}\mathrm{F}$ , and all three types proved satisfactory for holding pressures up to 60 psig.
Each valve was frozen and melted 100 times.
Additional experience was gained through operation of the valves incorporated in the Engineering Test Loop in Building 9201-3.
Figure 1 shows that the system (including the valve) was vertically mounted.
The total volume of the system was 5.6 gal.
The sump tank was made of 6-in. sched-40 Inconel pipe and had a volume of 2.2 gal.
A 3/8-in. sched-40 Inconel pipe connected the sump tank to the 1-l/2-in.
INOR-8 valve body by means of a 3/8- to 1-l/2-in.
The head tank was made of 6-in. sched-40 INOR-8; the volume of this tank was also 2.2 gal.
Average power required to maintain 1100 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the system was $4.5\mathrm{kw}$ .

The freeze-plug zone was cold formed to make a flat, 2 in. long with a $20^{\circ}$ angle of approach and discharge.
Inside dimensions were 1/2 in. x 2-1/4 in. x 2 in. long.
The induction coil was formed to permit preferential heating toward the outer edges of the freeze plug and to fit over the 2-in. pipe flat.
The coil occupied 6 in. of pipe length and was attached to the generator by standard brass tubing fittings and 1/4-in. copper tube.
The freeze plug was formed by directing controlled air flow in a 1-in. pipe between the heating coils to each of the flats.
The air nozzles were spaced 6 in. from the valve.

Two methods of supplying cooling air were employed: low-pressure air from a centrifugal blower piped to each valve flat with a 2-in. hose and high-pressure building air piped through a regulator and rotameter to each of the valve flats with a 1-in. pipe.
The same flattened pipe section (2-in. flats) was used by simply removing the induction coil and clamping a 1000-w $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ Calrod to each flat.
Calrods were bent into 6-in. long W-shaped units and clamped onto the pipe.
The valve was cycled 100 times without incident.
A flow of approx 8 scfm was required to maintain the plug.
The valve was cycled 100 times without difficulty and with no apparent damage to the pipe.
The average melt time was 35 sec at 12 kw.
Freeze time was 10 to 15 min with 50 to 60 cfm of air.
A flow of approx 8 scfm was required to maintain the plug.
The Calrod-heated valve was cycled 100 times with no difficulty.
The average melt time was 3 min with 1.6 kw input.
Again, an air flow of approx 8 scfm was required to maintain the plug.
The maximum Calrod sheath temperature attained in 3-1/2 min was $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: DISCUSSION OF TEST-VALVE INSTALLATION AND OPERATION
text: It was necessary to turn off these heaters (two 1000-w Calrods) during the melt cycle to prevent burnout when the direct-resistance heat was applied.

text: Since the thermocouples were located directly in or close to the cooling air stream, they gave a relative temperature reading only; however, once the plug size and freeze point had been established, the thermocouple 1-1/2 in. above the valve center was used as the frozen-condition indicator.

section: DESCRIPTION OF VALVE INSTALLATION IN ENGINEERING TEST LOOP
text: (2) To ensure that sufficient salt would remain in the valve to make a positive frozen seal under all conditions.

section: Freeze-Valve Operation And Results In The Engineering Test Loop
text: The valve to the flush tank in the Engineering Test Loop was operated with coolant salt (LiF-BeF $_2$ , 66-34 mole %) through 40 cycles without difficulty.

section: Freeze-Valve Operation And Results In The Engineering Test Loop
text: After approx 1300 hr of operation the initial melt appeared to fail to open the line, but indications are that the difficulty was a plug in the line upstream from the valve.
Gas flow was permitted to continue through the valve at the completion of the draining operation at a rate of 2.2 scfm helium.

section: DISCUSSION OF VALVE OPERATION IN THE ENGINEERING TEST LOOP
text: The lower cooling-air requirement for these valves compared with the earlier valves is attributed to the air nozzle being only 1 in. away from the valve flat and to the better air flow to the flat that is permitted by the open-center winding of the Calrods.
